bills.title: Laken Riley Act
bills.plain_title: Mandatory ICE Detention for Immigrants Charged with Crimes
bills.deck: Requires ICE to detain undocumented immigrants arrested or charged with theft or violent crimes — before any conviction.
bills.plain_summary: This law makes it mandatory for ICE to hold undocumented immigrants if they are even accused — not yet convicted — of crimes like shoplifting, burglary, or assault. Before this, ICE used its own judgment on who to detain. It was the very first bill President Trump signed in his second term, on January 29, 2025.

impacts.text: Detention based only on an arrest or charge — no conviction required.
Immigrants who've lived in the U.S. for years can be detained and deported for a misdemeanor accusation.
Supporters say it closes a loophole that let dangerous individuals avoid detention.
Republican-led states gain new legal standing to sue the federal government over immigration enforcement.

bills.who_affected: Undocumented immigrants, mixed-status families, immigration attorneys, state and local law enforcement.
bills.ticker_alert: ✅ SIGNED · Laken Riley Act — ICE must detain undocumented immigrants charged with theft, no conviction needed

bills.title: An act to provide for reconciliation pursuant to title II of H. Con. Res. 14.
bills.plain_title: Big Beautiful Bill: Tax Cuts and Spending Changes Act
bills.deck: Extends Trump-era tax cuts, increases defense and border spending, and cuts some social programs.
bills.plain_summary: This sweeping reconciliation law makes the 2017 Trump tax cuts permanent, adds new tax breaks for tips and overtime pay, boosts spending on border security and the military, and cuts hundreds of billions from Medicaid, food stamps, and student loan programs. It was driven by House Republicans and signed into law as Public Law 119-21. It is one of the largest tax and spending packages in recent American history.

impacts.text: Millions of low-income Americans could lose Medicaid coverage or SNAP food benefits due to new work requirements and eligibility cuts.
Most working Americans would see lower federal income tax bills as the 2017 tax cuts are made permanent, preventing a scheduled 2026 tax increase.
The law is projected to add trillions to the national debt over a decade, with supporters arguing growth will offset costs and critics disputing that.

bills.who_affected: Low-income families on Medicaid, SNAP recipients, tipped workers, overtime workers, small business owners, military families, undocumented immigrants, student loan borrowers, wealthy taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 🚨 Signed into law: Major tax cuts and social program reductions affecting millions

bills.title: Disapproving Biden SAVE Student Loan Rule
bills.plain_title: Canceling Biden's SAVE Student Loan Plan
bills.deck: Nullifies the Biden SAVE income-driven repayment plan, which capped payments at 5% of discretionary income. ~8 million borrowers in limbo.
bills.plain_summary: The SAVE plan calculated student loan payments as a percentage of your income and offered loan forgiveness faster than older programs. Congress cancelled it entirely and barred the Education Department from creating anything substantially similar without new legislation. About 8 million borrowers are now in repayment uncertainty.

impacts.text: ~8 million enrolled borrowers face uncertain repayment status and likely higher monthly payments.
Low-income borrowers who benefited most face the largest payment increases.
Supporters argue the plan was unconstitutional executive overreach.
Borrowers will be migrated to older income-driven plans with less favorable terms.

bills.who_affected: ~8 million student loan borrowers, recent college graduates, working-class and low-income borrowers.
bills.ticker_alert: ✅ SIGNED · Biden SAVE student loan plan cancelled — 8M borrowers face higher payments under older repayment rules

bills.title: Disapproving EPA Tailpipe Emissions Rule
bills.plain_title: Repealing Biden's Vehicle Emissions Standards
bills.deck: Nullifies Biden-era EPA rules requiring automakers to reduce tailpipe emissions by 2032 and bars the EPA from issuing similar rules.
bills.plain_summary: The Biden EPA had set rules requiring far lower vehicle emissions by 2032, pushing automakers toward electric vehicles. This law cancels those rules entirely and blocks the EPA from creating substantially similar regulations in the future without a new act of Congress.

impacts.text: Expected air quality improvements from stricter emissions standards are now cancelled.
Automakers no longer face the 2032 electrification compliance timeline.
Supporters say it prevents higher vehicle prices and protects consumer choice.
EPA is blocked from reissuing similar rules without congressional action.

bills.who_affected: All Americans (air quality), autoworkers, EV manufacturers, car buyers, climate advocates.
bills.ticker_alert: ✅ SIGNED · EPA tailpipe rule repealed — 2032 EV emissions targets cancelled, EPA blocked from similar rules

bills.title: Protecting Americans from Foreign Adversary Controlled Applications Act
bills.plain_title: The TikTok Divestiture Law
bills.deck: Forces ByteDance (TikTok's Chinese parent) to sell TikTok or face removal from U.S. app stores. Upheld by Supreme Court. Enforcement in flux.
bills.plain_summary: Passed in 2024 and upheld by the Supreme Court, this law required China's ByteDance to sell TikTok within ~9 months or face a ban. A sale has not been finalized. The Trump administration has signaled varying enforcement intentions, leaving the app's legal status uncertain for its 170 million U.S. users.

impacts.text: ~170 million American TikTok users face potential loss of access.
Content creators earning income through TikTok face loss of livelihood.
Proponents say it protects Americans from Chinese government data collection.
Sets precedent for government power to restrict access to a communications platform.

bills.who_affected: TikTok's 170M U.S. users, content creators, small businesses, digital rights advocates.
bills.ticker_alert: ⚠️ WATCH · TikTok law enforcement in flux — sale not finalized, 170M U.S. users in uncertainty

bills.title: To amend the FISA Amendments Act of 2008 to extend the authorities of title VII of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act of 1978 through April 30, 2026, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: To amend the FISA Amendments Act of 2008 to extend the authorities of title VII of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act of 1978 through April 30, 2026, and for other purposes.

bills.plain_summary: To amend the FISA Amendments Act of 2008 to extend the authorities of title VII of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act of 1978 through April 30, 2026, and for other purposes.. Status: Signed Law. Analysis unavailable.

bills.title: Taiwan Assurance Implementation Act
bills.plain_title: Taiwan Assurance Implementation Act
bills.deck: Became Public Law No: 119-45.
bills.plain_summary: Taiwan Assurance Implementation Act. Status: Signed Law.

bills.title: Department of Government Efficiency Authorization Act
bills.plain_title: Legalizing DOGE's Powers and Federal Data Access
bills.deck: Would formally authorize DOGE as a legal federal entity with official access to all federal agency databases, personnel records, and contracting systems.
bills.plain_summary: DOGE currently operates as an informal advisory body with contested legal authority. This bill would give it formal legal status and official access to virtually all federal agency databases. Critics argue it creates an unaccountable entity with sweeping access to sensitive data on millions of Americans.

impacts.text: Creates a presidentially-controlled body with access to sensitive personnel files without traditional oversight.
Federal employees could face termination recommendations outside normal civil service protections.
Supporters argue it could identify billions in wasteful government spending.
Sets precedent for executive efficiency bodies with broad data access rights.

bills.who_affected: 3+ million federal employees, government contractors, taxpayers, anyone whose data is in federal systems.
bills.ticker_alert: ⏳ IN COMMITTEE · DOGE Authorization Act — would give DOGE legal access to federal databases on 3M+ employees

bills.title: Pain-Capable Unborn Child Protection Act
bills.plain_title: Federal Abortion Ban After 15 Weeks
bills.deck: Would ban abortions nationwide after 15 weeks — overriding state laws that permit abortions beyond that point. Limited exceptions only.
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a federal ban on abortions after 15 weeks, overriding the laws of 20+ states that currently allow them beyond that point. It has no exception for severe fetal abnormalities incompatible with life, and no exception for the health (as opposed to life) of the mother.

impacts.text: Overrides more permissive laws in 20+ states that currently allow abortions beyond 15 weeks.
No exception for fetal abnormalities incompatible with life.
Physicians face criminal penalties — could deter OB/GYN providers broadly.
Supporters say it aligns U.S. law with many European countries' gestational limits.
Would face immediate federal court challenges.

bills.who_affected: Women of reproductive age, OB/GYN physicians, reproductive health clinics, states with current abortion access laws.
bills.ticker_alert: 🔴 WATCH · H.R.7 Federal Abortion Ban — 15-week nationwide limit would override laws in 20+ states

bills.title: One Big Beautiful Bill Act
bills.plain_title: Sweeping Tax Cuts, Medicaid Cuts & Spending Overhaul
bills.deck: Makes 2017 Trump tax cuts permanent, eliminates taxes on tips and overtime, and cuts Medicaid, SNAP, and student loan programs.
bills.plain_summary: This massive bill permanently extends the 2017 Trump tax cuts (which otherwise expire in 2025), adds new tax breaks for tipped workers and overtime pay, and funds it by cutting billions from Medicaid, food stamps (SNAP), and student loan forgiveness. The Congressional Budget Office estimates it adds $3–5 trillion to the national debt over 10 years.

impacts.text: CBO estimates 8–13 million Americans could lose Medicaid health coverage.
~40 million SNAP recipients face reduced food assistance.
No federal income tax on tips — benefits millions of service and hospitality workers.
No federal income tax on overtime pay.
Trillions added to national debt could raise interest rates and reduce purchasing power.

bills.who_affected: Virtually all Americans — especially Medicaid recipients, food stamp households, restaurant workers, and taxpayers.
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 MOVING · H.R.1 'One Big Beautiful Bill' — would cut Medicaid for up to 13M Americans while eliminating taxes on tips

bills.title: Farm, Food, and National Security Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: 2026 Farm Bill Reauthorizing Agriculture and Food Programs
bills.deck: Reauthorizes major U.S. farm, food assistance, and rural development programs for the next several years.
bills.plain_summary: The Farm, Food, and National Security Act of 2026 is a comprehensive farm bill that reauthorizes and updates federal programs covering crop insurance, food stamps (SNAP), conservation, rural development, and agricultural trade. It was advanced out of the House Agriculture Committee by a vote of 34 to 17. The bill now moves toward a full House vote and must still pass the Senate before becoming law.

impacts.text: Critics warn the bill may cut SNAP food assistance benefits, reducing food access for low-income families and children who rely on the program.
Farmers gain extended crop insurance protections and updated commodity support payments, providing more financial stability against weather disasters and market swings.

The bill reshapes how conservation funding is distributed, shifting some climate-focused agricultural dollars toward broader land management priorities — a change praised by some and criticized by others.

bills.who_affected: Farmers and ranchers, SNAP recipients and low-income families, rural communities, grocery shoppers, conservation groups, agricultural businesses, food banks
bills.ticker_alert: 🌾 House farm bill advances, affecting food aid and farm support for millions

bills.title: Establishing the congressional budget for the United States Government for fiscal year 2025 and setting forth the appropriate budgetary levels for fiscal years 2026 through 2034.
bills.plain_title: Federal Budget Plan for 2025 Through 2034
bills.deck: Sets the federal government's spending and revenue targets for 2025, with projections through 2034.
bills.plain_summary: This bill establishes the official federal budget framework for 2025 and outlines spending and revenue goals for the next decade. It was passed by the Republican-led House as a concurrent resolution, meaning it requires Senate agreement but does not need the President's signature. It has been sent back for a formal reprint after committee review, meaning it is still in the early stages of the congressional budget process.

impacts.text: Deep proposed spending cuts could reduce or eliminate federal programs many Americans rely on, including Medicaid, food assistance, and education funding.
Supporters say reducing deficit spending over ten years could slow national debt growth, potentially lowering long-term interest costs for taxpayers.
This resolution sets targets but does not itself change any law — actual cuts or tax changes require separate legislation that Congress still must pass.

bills.who_affected: Low-income families, Medicaid recipients, federal program beneficiaries, military personnel, taxpayers, small business owners, retirees on Social Security
bills.ticker_alert: 📊 Congress proposes 10-year budget blueprint with major spending cut targets

bills.title: Department of Homeland Security Appropriations Act, 2026
bills.plain_title: Homeland Security Budget Funding Bill for 2026
bills.deck: Sets the annual budget for the Department of Homeland Security, covering border security, FEMA, and other agencies.
bills.plain_summary: This bill determines how much money the Department of Homeland Security receives and how it can be spent in fiscal year 2026. It covers funding for agencies like CBP, ICE, FEMA, TSA, and the Coast Guard. The bill has passed the House and is currently awaiting action in the Senate Appropriations Committee.

impacts.text: Critics warn that funding levels for immigration enforcement could expand detention and deportation programs, affecting immigrant families and communities.
Disaster relief funding through FEMA helps millions of Americans recover from hurricanes, wildfires, floods, and other natural disasters.
Border security and immigration enforcement spending levels remain contested, with sharp disagreements between parties over priorities and effectiveness.

bills.who_affected: Immigrants and asylum seekers, disaster survivors, air travelers, coastal communities, federal employees, border communities, emergency responders
bills.ticker_alert: 🏛️ House passes 2026 Homeland Security budget; Senate review pending

bills.title: DIGNIDAD (Dignity) Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Comprehensive Immigration Reform and Border Security Act
bills.deck: Creates a path to legal status for millions of undocumented immigrants while tightening border security and reforming the immigration system.
bills.plain_summary: The DIGNIDAD Act of 2025 is a sweeping immigration reform bill that would create legal pathways for undocumented immigrants already living in the U.S., including Dreamers and farmworkers, while also investing in border security and modernizing the immigration court system. The bill addresses multiple aspects of immigration policy, from worker visas to asylum processes. It has been referred to several House committees and has not yet received a floor vote.

impacts.text: Critics argue it could incentivize future illegal immigration by offering legal status to those who entered without authorization, and may strain public services.
Millions of long-term undocumented residents, including Dreamers brought as children, could gain legal status and work authorization, reducing family separation.
The bill would significantly restructure the immigration court system and visa allocation rules, with uncertain effects on wait times and legal immigration backlogs.

bills.who_affected: Undocumented immigrants, DACA recipients, farmworkers, asylum seekers, U.S. employers relying on immigrant labor, border communities, immigration lawyers, U.S.-born family members of undocumented residents
bills.ticker_alert: 🗽 DIGNIDAD Act proposes legal status for millions of undocumented U.S. residents

bills.title: Energy Dominance Act
bills.plain_title: Fast-Tracking Oil, Gas & Mining Permits on Federal Land
bills.deck: Streamlines permitting for oil, gas, coal, and mineral extraction on federal lands — cutting review timelines and limiting environmental legal challenges.
bills.plain_summary: Getting permits to drill or mine on federal land currently takes years due to environmental review requirements. This bill caps those timelines, limits legal challenges by environmental groups, and expands areas of federal land open to energy development.

impacts.text: Faster permitting reduces environmental review time, increasing risk of poorly-assessed projects.
Limits the ability of communities and environmental groups to challenge projects.
Lower energy production costs could reduce gas and energy prices for consumers.
Could create energy and mining jobs in rural areas.
Expands fossil fuel extraction on public lands, accelerating greenhouse gas emissions.

bills.who_affected: Rural communities near federal lands, energy industry workers, environmental advocates, public land users.
bills.ticker_alert: ⏳ IN COMMITTEE · Energy Dominance Act — would fast-track oil and mining permits on federal land

bills.title: Safeguard American Voter Eligibility Act
bills.plain_title: Proof of Citizenship Required to Register to Vote
bills.deck: Requires all Americans to provide documentary proof of citizenship — passport, birth certificate, or equivalent — when registering to vote.
bills.plain_summary: Currently, registering to vote only requires signing a form swearing you're a citizen. This bill requires physical proof — a passport, birth certificate, or naturalization papers. Supporters say it prevents non-citizen voting. Critics say millions of eligible Americans lack these documents and would be blocked from registering.

impacts.text: Millions of eligible voters without passports or birth certificates could be blocked from registering.
Disproportionately affects low-income, elderly, and rural Americans.
Supporters say it ensures only citizens cast ballots in federal elections.
Non-citizen voting in federal elections is already illegal and extremely rare.

bills.who_affected: All unregistered Americans, naturalized citizens, elderly voters, low-income and rural households.
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 PASSED HOUSE · SAVE Act — proof of citizenship required to register to vote, now heads to Senate

bills.title: Gas Prices Relief Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Tax Relief to Lower Gas Prices for Americans
bills.deck: This bill would temporarily suspend the federal gas tax to lower prices at the pump.
bills.plain_summary: The Gas Prices Relief Act of 2026 proposes to temporarily suspend the federal excise tax on gasoline and diesel fuel, which currently adds about 18-24 cents per gallon to the price Americans pay at the pump. By removing this tax for a set period, the bill aims to provide direct cost relief to drivers and households struggling with high fuel costs. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Suspending the gas tax could reduce the Highway Trust Fund by billions of dollars, potentially delaying road, bridge, and infrastructure repairs across the country.
Drivers could save 18 to 24 cents per gallon at the pump, providing meaningful short-term relief to commuters, small businesses, and rural Americans who drive long distances.
Economists debate whether gas tax suspensions actually lower consumer prices, since oil companies and retailers may absorb some or all of the savings rather than passing them on to drivers.

bills.who_affected: everyday drivers, rural Americans, trucking and delivery companies, small businesses, road construction workers, commuters, low-income households
bills.ticker_alert: ⛽ Bill proposes suspending federal gas tax to cut pump prices for all drivers

bills.title: CHILD Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Child Health Insurance and Dental Coverage Expansion Act
bills.deck: This bill would expand dental and health coverage options for children enrolled in Medicaid and CHIP programs.
bills.plain_summary: The CHILD Act of 2025 aims to strengthen health and dental care access for low-income children covered under Medicaid and the Children's Health Insurance Program (CHIP). The bill has been placed on the Senate Legislative Calendar, meaning it is eligible for a floor vote but has not yet passed. It targets gaps in coverage that leave many children without adequate preventive and routine care.

impacts.text: Expanding coverage mandates could increase federal and state Medicaid spending, potentially straining budgets or requiring new funding offsets.
Millions of low-income children could gain access to dental and health services they currently lack, improving long-term health outcomes.
The bill's effectiveness depends on whether enough dentists and providers accept Medicaid patients, a persistent access problem in many states.

bills.who_affected: Low-income children, families enrolled in Medicaid and CHIP, pediatric dentists and doctors, state Medicaid administrators, taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 🦷 Senate bill aims to expand dental and health care for millions of low-income children

bills.title: Safeguarding Women from Chemical Abortion Act
bills.plain_title: Restricting Mifepristone Access for Medication Abortions
bills.deck: This bill would impose new federal restrictions on the prescribing and dispensing of mifepristone, a drug used in medication abortions.
bills.plain_summary: The Safeguarding Women from Chemical Abortion Act would place stricter federal regulations on mifepristone, the most commonly used medication abortion drug in the United States. The bill would likely require in-person dispensing, limit who can prescribe it, and add new reporting requirements. It has been referred to the House committees on Energy and Commerce and the Judiciary, where it awaits further consideration.

impacts.text: Women in rural or underserved areas could lose access to medication abortion, which currently accounts for over half of all U.S. abortions.
Supporters say stricter oversight of mifepristone prescribing could improve patient safety monitoring and adverse event reporting.
The bill reignites the ongoing legal and political debate over federal authority to regulate abortion-related drugs approved by the FDA.

bills.who_affected: Women of reproductive age, abortion providers, OB-GYN physicians, rural healthcare patients, pharmacists, telehealth providers
bills.ticker_alert: 🚫 House bill seeks new federal restrictions on medication abortion drug mifepristone

bills.title: Equal Representation Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Count Only Citizens in Congressional Apportionment
bills.deck: This bill would exclude non-citizens from the population count used to divide congressional seats among states.
bills.plain_summary: The Equal Representation Act of 2025 would require that only U.S. citizens — not all residents — be counted when deciding how many congressional seats and Electoral College votes each state receives. Currently, the Census counts everyone living in the U.S., including non-citizens. The bill passed out of committee by a razor-thin 20-19 vote and now moves to the full House for consideration.

impacts.text: States with large immigrant populations (like California, Texas, and New York) could lose congressional seats and Electoral College votes, reducing their political representation in Washington.
Supporters say it ensures only those eligible to vote determine congressional representation, giving states with fewer non-citizens a greater proportional voice in Congress.
The Census Bureau has never used a citizenship-only count for apportionment; implementing this change would require new data collection methods and could face significant legal challenges.

bills.who_affected: Non-citizen immigrants, residents of high-immigration states, voters in low-immigration states, state legislatures drawing district maps, Electoral College voters
bills.ticker_alert: 🗳️ Bill would cut congressional seats in states with large immigrant populations

bills.title: Momnibus Act
bills.plain_title: Maternal Health Omnibus Act for Moms and Babies
bills.deck: A sweeping bill to reduce maternal mortality and improve health outcomes for mothers across America.
bills.plain_summary: The Momnibus Act is a comprehensive package of policies aimed at reducing the maternal mortality crisis in the United States, particularly for Black and Native American women who die from pregnancy-related causes at far higher rates. The bill addresses issues ranging from mental health, housing, and rural care access to data collection and workforce training. It was referred to multiple House committees, meaning it is still in early legislative stages and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Critics warn the bill's broad scope and high cost could strain federal budgets without guaranteeing measurable improvements in maternal outcomes.
Could significantly reduce maternal death rates, especially for Black and Indigenous women, by expanding access to midwives, doulas, and mental health services.
The bill bundles many separate policy areas — housing, immigration, environment, veterans — making it harder to pass as a single package but allowing for broad coalition support.

bills.who_affected: Pregnant women, new mothers, Black and Indigenous women, rural communities, veterans who are mothers, midwives and doulas, Medicaid recipients, maternal healthcare providers
bills.ticker_alert: 🤱 Momnibus Act targets maternal mortality crisis affecting millions of American mothers

bills.title: Deceptive Practices and Voter Intimidation Prevention Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Ban on Voter Misinformation and Election Intimidation
bills.deck: This bill makes it a federal crime to spread false information or intimidate voters during elections.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would make it illegal to knowingly spread false information about voting — such as wrong dates, locations, or eligibility rules — that could prevent people from voting. It also strengthens penalties for intimidating or threatening voters. The bill was introduced in the House and has been referred to the House Judiciary Committee, where it awaits further action.

impacts.text: Critics warn broad definitions of 'deceptive practices' could be used to prosecute political speech or satire, raising First Amendment concerns.
Voters would have stronger federal protections against deliberate misinformation campaigns designed to suppress turnout or misdirect them on election rules.
Enforcement would rely on the Justice Department, raising questions about how consistently the law would be applied across different political contexts.

bills.who_affected: Voters in federal elections, political campaigns and operatives, social media platforms, civil rights organizations, law enforcement agencies
bills.ticker_alert: 🗳️ New bill would make spreading false voting information a federal crime

bills.title: John R. Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Restoring Federal Oversight of State Voting Laws
bills.deck: Requires states with a history of voting discrimination to get federal approval before changing election rules.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would restore and strengthen key parts of the Voting Rights Act that were weakened by Supreme Court decisions in 2013 and 2021. It would require certain states and counties with a history of voting discrimination to get federal approval before changing their voting laws, a process called 'preclearance.' The bill was introduced in the Senate and has been referred to the Judiciary Committee, where it awaits further action.

impacts.text: States and localities could face costly delays and federal interference when updating legitimate election administration laws.
Minority voters in areas with documented discrimination histories gain an early legal shield against potentially discriminatory voting rule changes.
Which states and counties must seek federal approval is determined by a new coverage formula, a deeply contested political and legal question.

bills.who_affected: Black voters, Hispanic voters, Native American voters, Asian American voters, state election officials, county governments, civil rights organizations, federal courts
bills.ticker_alert: 🗳️ Senate bill seeks to restore federal voting rights oversight for key states

bills.title: CRACKDOWN Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Cracking Down on Fentanyl and Drug Trafficking
bills.deck: This bill increases penalties and enforcement tools to combat illegal drug trafficking, especially fentanyl, into the United States.
bills.plain_summary: The CRACKDOWN Act of 2026 (HR 7721) aims to strengthen the federal government's ability to fight drug trafficking by increasing criminal penalties and expanding law enforcement authorities, with a particular focus on fentanyl and other synthetic opioids. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has cleared committee and is awaiting a full House vote. It reflects ongoing congressional efforts to address the opioid crisis that has claimed hundreds of thousands of American lives.

impacts.text: Critics warn that harsher mandatory minimums could disproportionately imprison low-level offenders, particularly from minority communities, repeating past failures of the War on Drugs.
Stronger penalties and enforcement tools could deter large-scale fentanyl traffickers and potentially reduce the drug supply fueling tens of thousands of overdose deaths annually.
The bill's effectiveness depends heavily on implementation — debate continues over whether stricter sentencing or treatment-focused approaches better reduce drug-related harm.

bills.who_affected: Drug trafficking defendants, federal law enforcement agencies, communities hardest hit by the opioid crisis, addiction treatment advocates, civil liberties organizations, border security personnel
bills.ticker_alert: 🚨 House bill targets fentanyl traffickers with tougher federal penalties

bills.title: Child Care for Working Families Act
bills.plain_title: Affordable Child Care Expansion for Working Families
bills.deck: This bill would cap child care costs at 7% of income and expand federal subsidies to millions more families.
bills.plain_summary: The Child Care for Working Families Act would make child care affordable by limiting what working families pay to no more than 7% of their income, with the federal government covering the rest through expanded subsidies. It also aims to raise wages for child care workers and increase the availability of care for infants and toddlers. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: The bill's estimated cost of hundreds of billions of dollars could increase the federal deficit or require tax increases to fund.
Families currently spending $15,000–$30,000 per year on child care could see their costs drop dramatically, freeing up income for other needs.
Critics and supporters debate whether large federal subsidies will reduce prices or simply increase demand in ways that raise costs further.

bills.who_affected: Working parents with young children, low- and middle-income families, child care workers, child care center operators, taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 👶 Bill would cap child care costs at 7% of family income nationwide

bills.title: PROTECT Kids Act
bills.plain_title: Child Online Safety and Social Media Age Verification Act
bills.deck: Requires age verification and parental consent for minors using social media and online platforms.
bills.plain_summary: The PROTECT Kids Act would require online platforms and social media companies to verify users' ages and obtain parental consent before allowing children to create accounts or access certain content. It places new legal obligations on tech companies to implement safeguards protecting minors online. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has cleared committee and is awaiting a vote in the full House of Representatives.

impacts.text: Age verification requirements could expose all users' personal identity data to potential breaches or misuse by platforms collecting that information.
Children could gain stronger protections from predators, harmful content, and addictive platform features designed to exploit young users.
Tech companies would face significant compliance costs, which could be passed to consumers or reduce free services while smaller platforms struggle more than large ones.

bills.who_affected: Children and teenagers under 18, parents and guardians, social media companies, app developers, digital advertisers, child safety advocates
bills.ticker_alert: 🛡️ House bill would require age checks before kids join social media

bills.title: Providing for consideration of the bill (H.R. 8035) to amend the FISA Amendments Act of 2008 to extend the authorities of title VII of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act of 1978 through October 20, 2027, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: Extension of Foreign Surveillance Authority Through 2027
bills.deck: This resolution set the rules for debating a bill extending government foreign intelligence surveillance powers until 2027.
bills.plain_summary: This House Resolution established the procedures and time limits for debating H.R. 8035, which renews Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act — a law allowing the U.S. government to collect communications of foreign targets overseas, including messages with Americans. The underlying bill extends these surveillance authorities through October 2027. The resolution has completed its legislative action in the House.

impacts.text: Americans' communications with foreign contacts can be collected without a warrant, raising privacy concerns for millions of ordinary citizens
U.S. intelligence agencies retain a key legal tool used to detect and disrupt terrorism, espionage, and cyberattacks against Americans
The extension delays a longer congressional debate over whether stronger privacy safeguards or court oversight should be added to the surveillance program

bills.who_affected: American citizens who communicate with people abroad, journalists, lawyers, activists, immigrants with foreign family ties, U.S. intelligence and national security agencies, civil liberties advocates
bills.ticker_alert: 📡 House advances rule to extend warrantless foreign surveillance powers through 2027

bills.title: Time to Choose Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Bill Requiring Citizens to Prove Voting Eligibility
bills.deck: Would require voters to prove U.S. citizenship before registering to vote in federal elections.
bills.plain_summary: The Time to Choose Act of 2025 would require people to provide documentary proof of U.S. citizenship, such as a passport or birth certificate, when registering to vote in federal elections. It was introduced in the Senate and has been referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs, meaning it has not yet advanced to a full vote. The bill aims to ensure only eligible citizens cast ballots in federal races.

impacts.text: Millions of eligible citizens who lack easy access to passports or birth certificates could be effectively blocked from registering to vote.
Supporters say it would strengthen confidence in election integrity by creating a clear documentary standard for citizenship verification.
States already have varying voter registration requirements, so this bill would create a new federal minimum standard whose enforcement and cost burden on states remains debated.

bills.who_affected: All U.S. voters and prospective registrants, low-income Americans, rural residents, elderly citizens, tribal communities, naturalized citizens, election administrators
bills.ticker_alert: 🗳️ Senate bill would require citizenship documents to register for federal elections

bills.title: Making appropriations for military construction, the Department of Veterans Affairs, and related agencies for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2027, and for other purposes.
bills.plain_title: Military Construction and Veterans Affairs Budget Bill 2027
bills.deck: Sets the federal budget for military bases, veteran healthcare, and related programs for fiscal year 2027.
bills.plain_summary: This bill allocates federal funding for military construction projects, the Department of Veterans Affairs, and related agencies for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2027. It covers veteran healthcare, benefits, housing for troops, and base infrastructure improvements. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Funding caps may leave some VA hospitals understaffed or delay construction of new veteran care facilities in high-demand areas.
Millions of veterans could see continued or expanded access to healthcare, mental health services, and disability benefits funded by this appropriation.
Military construction spending directs billions to base infrastructure, which critics call pork-barrel spending but supporters say boosts local economies near bases.

bills.who_affected: Veterans, active-duty military personnel, military families, VA employees, defense contractors, communities near military bases
bills.ticker_alert: 🏥 House bill funds veteran healthcare and military bases through 2027

bills.title: KIDS Act
bills.plain_title: Kids Online Safety and Data Privacy Act
bills.deck: Requires stricter data privacy protections and safety standards for children using online platforms.
bills.plain_summary: The KIDS Act would strengthen online protections for minors by requiring tech companies to limit data collection on children, restrict harmful content algorithms targeting kids, and enforce stronger age verification measures. The bill was referred to the House Committees on Energy and Commerce and the Judiciary, where it awaits further consideration. It has not yet been voted on by the full House.

impacts.text: Tech companies may pass compliance costs to consumers, and stricter age verification could limit access to platforms for teens and young adults
Children would have stronger legal protections against data harvesting and algorithmically pushed harmful content on social media and apps
Debate exists over whether parental consent requirements or platform-side restrictions are the more effective and less burdensome approach to child safety

bills.who_affected: Children and teenagers under 18, parents and guardians, social media companies, app developers, advertisers targeting youth audiences
bills.ticker_alert: 🛡️ KIDS Act targets Big Tech data collection and harmful content affecting millions of children

bills.title: Financial Services and General Government Appropriations Act, 2027
bills.plain_title: Federal Agency Funding Bill for Fiscal Year 2027
bills.deck: Sets annual spending levels for the Treasury, IRS, SEC, federal courts, and other key government agencies.
bills.plain_summary: This bill allocates funding for a wide range of federal agencies including the IRS, Securities and Exchange Commission, federal judiciary, Small Business Administration, and the Executive Office of the President for fiscal year 2027. It determines how much money these agencies have to operate, enforce laws, and serve the public. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has cleared committee and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Cuts or flat funding to the IRS could reduce enforcement on wealthy tax cheats while leaving middle-class filers with fewer customer service resources.
Funding the Small Business Administration helps millions of small business owners access loans, disaster relief, and entrepreneurship programs.
The bill's IRS funding level is contested — supporters say reductions protect taxpayers from over-enforcement, while critics warn it increases the deficit by reducing tax collections.

bills.who_affected: Taxpayers, small business owners, federal employees, investors, federal court users, government contractors, recipients of federal financial assistance
bills.ticker_alert: 💰 House bill sets 2027 budgets for IRS, SEC, courts & more

bills.title: No Tax on Social Security Benefits Act
bills.plain_title: Eliminating Federal Taxes on Social Security Benefits
bills.deck: Would eliminate the federal income tax on Social Security benefits entirely — currently up to 85% of benefits are taxable for higher-income recipients.
bills.plain_summary: If you receive Social Security and have income above ~$25,000/year, you currently pay federal income tax on up to 85% of your benefits. This bill makes all Social Security income tax-free. The cost is ~$1.6 trillion in federal revenue over 10 years, which could accelerate Social Security's insolvency.

impacts.text: ~70 million Social Security recipients would pay less in federal taxes.
Largest benefit for middle-income retirees who currently pay tax on benefits.
Reduces federal revenue by ~$1.6 trillion over 10 years.
Could accelerate Social Security Trust Fund insolvency.

bills.who_affected: All Social Security recipients (70+ million), future retirees, and taxpayers who fund the program.
bills.ticker_alert: 🆕 INTRODUCED · No Tax on Social Security — would make all SS benefits tax-free for 70M recipients

bills.title: Protecting Children on Social Media Act
bills.plain_title: Age Verification Required for Social Media — Under 13 Banned
bills.deck: Requires social media platforms to verify ages of all new users and prohibits children under 13, with parental consent required for ages 13–17.
bills.plain_summary: Social media companies would be required to use age verification tools for all new users. Children under 13 couldn't create accounts. Teens 13–17 would need verified parental permission. Privacy advocates warn that age verification at scale requires collecting sensitive personal data from every user.

impacts.text: Stronger legal protection for children from predatory content and adult interactions online.
Age verification requires collecting sensitive ID documents from all users, not just minors.
Significant compliance costs could disadvantage smaller platforms vs. Facebook and Instagram.
Could limit teen access to peer support forums and mental health communities online.

bills.who_affected: All social media users (everyone must be age-verified), parents, children, tech companies, privacy advocates.
bills.ticker_alert: ⏳ IN COMMITTEE · Social media age verification bill — under-13 ban, parental consent required for teens

bills.title: No Tax on Tips Act
bills.plain_title: Eliminating Federal Taxes on Tip Income
bills.deck: Eliminates federal income taxes on tips received by workers in industries where tipping is customary — restaurants, hotels, salons, rideshare.
bills.plain_summary: Workers in tipped professions would pay zero federal income tax on tips. You'd still pay taxes on your base hourly wage. The average estimated benefit for full-time tipped workers is $1,700–$4,000 per year. The bill would reduce federal revenue by an estimated $100–150 billion over 10 years.

impacts.text: Workers in tipped professions take home significantly more of their earnings.
Benefits are uneven — servers at upscale restaurants gain more than those at diners.
Reduces federal revenue by ~$100–150 billion over 10 years.
Could incentivize employers to restructure wages as tips to exploit the exemption.

bills.who_affected: ~4 million tipped workers in food service, hospitality, beauty, and transportation sectors.
bills.ticker_alert: ⏳ IN COMMITTEE · No Tax on Tips Act — could eliminate federal taxes on tip income for 4M service workers

bills.title: PROVE IT Act
bills.plain_title: Carbon Border Fee on High-Pollution Imports
bills.deck: Creates fees on imports from countries with weaker carbon standards, leveling the playing field for American manufacturers.
bills.plain_summary: U.S. companies face environmental rules that raise production costs. Foreign competitors face fewer restrictions. This bill adds a fee to imported goods based on their carbon footprint, making U.S.-made products more price-competitive. Revenue would support American workers in affected industries.

impacts.text: Could modestly raise consumer prices on imported steel, aluminum, cement, and fertilizer.
Levels the competitive playing field for U.S. manufacturers following environmental rules.
Creates financial incentive for other countries to reduce pollution.
Trading partners could retaliate with tariffs on U.S. exports.

bills.who_affected: U.S. manufacturers, importers, consumers, steel and aluminum industries, global trade relationships.
bills.ticker_alert: ⏳ IN COMMITTEE · PROVE IT Act — carbon border fee on imports from high-pollution countries

bills.title: Stop Terror-Financing and Tax Penalties on American Hostages Act
bills.plain_title: IRS Can Revoke Nonprofit Tax Status Without Court Review
bills.deck: Allows the IRS to revoke nonprofit tax-exempt status from organizations designated as 'terrorist supporting' — with 90 days' notice and limited judicial review.
bills.plain_summary: This bill gives the Treasury Secretary power to label nonprofits as 'terrorist supporting' and strip their tax-exempt status with very limited court challenge options. Civil liberties groups warn the broad definition could target political opponents, charities, or universities.

impacts.text: Civil liberties groups warn it could target political opposition, journalism orgs, or advocacy groups.
Very limited due process — 90 days' notice with restricted court challenge options.
Supporters say it closes a genuine terror financing loophole.
Could affect universities, international aid organizations, and think tanks.

bills.who_affected: Nonprofits, charities, universities, advocacy organizations, donors, press freedom groups.
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 PASSED HOUSE · IRS nonprofit tax status bill — Treasury could strip tax-exempt status without conviction

bills.title: Lulu’s Law
bills.plain_title: Bill to Protect Children from Online Sexual Exploitation
bills.deck: Requires online platforms to better detect and report child sexual abuse material to protect minors.
bills.plain_summary: Lulu's Law would strengthen requirements for tech companies and online platforms to detect, report, and remove child sexual abuse material (CSAM) from their services. The bill is named in honor of a child victim and aims to close loopholes in existing reporting obligations. It has been received in the Senate and is currently held at the desk, meaning it has not yet been assigned to a committee for review.

impacts.text: Tech companies may face costly compliance burdens, potentially passing expenses to users or reducing smaller platforms' ability to operate
Children could be better protected from online sexual exploitation through stricter detection and faster removal of abuse material
Expanded surveillance and scanning requirements raise ongoing debate about user privacy versus child safety obligations

bills.who_affected: Children and minors, parents of minors, tech and social media companies, child safety advocates, privacy advocates, law enforcement agencies
bills.ticker_alert: 🛡️ Lulu's Law targets online child exploitation, tightening platform reporting rules

bills.title: Lulu’s Law
bills.plain_title: Expanding Mental Health Crisis Services for Children
bills.deck: Requires states to include pediatric mental health crisis care in Medicaid coverage for children.
bills.plain_summary: Lulu's Law would require state Medicaid programs to cover mental health crisis services for children and adolescents, ensuring young people can access emergency behavioral health care. The bill is named after a child whose death highlighted gaps in pediatric mental health crisis services. It has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has passed committee and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: States may face increased Medicaid costs and administrative burdens to implement new mandatory pediatric crisis service requirements.
Low-income children experiencing mental health crises would gain guaranteed access to specialized emergency behavioral health care through Medicaid.
The scope and quality of covered services could vary by state depending on how each interprets and implements the federal mandate.

bills.who_affected: Children and adolescents on Medicaid, low-income families, pediatric mental health providers, state Medicaid administrators, child welfare agencies
bills.ticker_alert: 🧠 Bill would guarantee mental health crisis care for Medicaid-enrolled children

bills.title: SCOPE Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Expanding Broadband Access and Closing Coverage Gaps
bills.deck: This bill aims to improve broadband internet coverage maps and expand high-speed internet access across underserved areas of the U.S.
bills.plain_summary: The SCOPE Act of 2026 (HR 7684) seeks to strengthen broadband coverage mapping and improve how the government identifies communities that lack reliable high-speed internet. It was referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce, where it awaits further review. The bill is intended to help ensure federal broadband funding reaches the Americans who need it most, particularly in rural and low-income communities.

impacts.text: More detailed mapping requirements could slow down broadband deployment funding by adding bureaucratic steps before money reaches communities.
Better broadband maps could direct billions in federal funding more accurately to rural and low-income Americans who currently lack reliable internet access.
The bill's effectiveness depends heavily on how federal agencies implement the mapping rules and whether local governments participate in data collection.

bills.who_affected: Rural residents, low-income households, small businesses in underserved areas, internet service providers, state broadband offices, tribal communities
bills.ticker_alert: 📡 SCOPE Act targets better broadband maps to fix digital divide for millions

bills.title: E-Access Act
bills.plain_title: Electronic Access to Government Documents Act
bills.deck: Requires federal agencies to make public documents available online for free.
bills.plain_summary: The E-Access Act would require federal agencies to publish government documents and records online in accessible digital formats at no cost to the public. This bill aims to make it easier for ordinary Americans to find and read official government information without paying fees or visiting offices in person. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process.

impacts.text: Federal agencies may face significant costs and staffing burdens to digitize and maintain large volumes of existing documents online.
Everyday Americans would gain free, convenient online access to government records that currently require fees or in-person requests.
The bill could shift how agencies allocate IT and administrative budgets, potentially affecting other government services depending on implementation.

bills.who_affected: General public, journalists, researchers, small business owners, legal professionals, people with disabilities, low-income Americans without means to pay record fees
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 New bill would make federal government documents free and online for all Americans

bills.title: Stop Illegal Alien Cops Act
bills.plain_title: Ban on Non-Citizens Serving as Police Officers
bills.deck: This bill would prohibit non-U.S. citizens from working as law enforcement officers anywhere in the United States.
bills.plain_summary: The Stop Illegal Alien Cops Act would make it illegal for anyone who is not a U.S. citizen to serve as a police officer or law enforcement official at the federal, state, or local level. Some cities and states have recently changed their laws to allow non-citizens, including undocumented immigrants, to join police forces to address officer shortages. This bill was referred to the House Judiciary Committee, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Law enforcement agencies already facing staffing shortages could lose eligible recruits, potentially leaving communities with fewer officers on patrol.
Supporters argue ensuring all officers are U.S. citizens strengthens national security and public trust in law enforcement institutions.
The bill's effect depends heavily on how many non-citizen officers currently serve and whether states would be legally compelled to comply with a federal mandate.

bills.who_affected: Non-citizen legal residents seeking law enforcement careers, local and state police departments, communities with officer shortages, undocumented immigrants, taxpayers funding public safety
bills.ticker_alert: 🚨 Bill introduced to ban non-citizens from serving as police officers nationwide

bills.title: CLEAR Path Act
bills.plain_title: Streamlining Nuclear Energy Licensing and Permitting
bills.deck: This bill reforms federal processes to make it faster and cheaper to build new nuclear power plants in the U.S.
bills.plain_summary: The CLEAR Path Act (Creating Helpful Incentives to Produce Semiconductors... no — Clearing Legislative Enhancements for Advanced Reactors) reforms the Nuclear Regulatory Commission's licensing and permitting procedures to reduce delays and costs for building new nuclear reactors, including advanced reactor designs. It aims to modernize outdated regulatory frameworks that critics say make U.S. nuclear energy uncompetitive. The bill has been placed on the Senate Legislative Calendar, meaning it awaits a floor vote.

impacts.text: Critics argue streamlined licensing could reduce safety review time for nuclear facilities, potentially increasing risks to nearby communities.
Faster nuclear plant approvals could lower energy costs long-term and help the U.S. meet clean energy goals without relying solely on fossil fuels.
The bill shifts regulatory authority and timelines at the NRC, which supporters call modernization and opponents call a weakening of oversight.

bills.who_affected: Energy ratepayers, nuclear industry workers, environmental advocates, communities near nuclear facilities, utility companies, clean energy investors
bills.ticker_alert: ⚡ Senate bill would fast-track permits for new U.S. nuclear power plants

bills.title: Carlton H. Ingram Veterans’ Benefits Improvement Act
bills.plain_title: Veterans Benefits Improvement and Modernization Act
bills.deck: This bill proposes updates to how veterans receive and appeal their federal benefits.
bills.plain_summary: The Carlton H. Ingram Veterans' Benefits Improvement Act (HR 8066) aims to improve how the Department of Veterans Affairs delivers and processes benefits to veterans, including potential changes to claims, appeals, or payment processes. The bill is named after Carlton H. Ingram, honoring a figure connected to veterans' service. It has been referred to the House Committee on Veterans' Affairs, meaning it is in the early stages of consideration and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Implementation costs could divert VA funding from direct veteran healthcare or services if not separately appropriated.
Veterans who have struggled with slow or denied benefits claims could see faster decisions and improved access to earned benefits.
The bill's actual scope and specific provisions are not fully detailed in public summaries, making it difficult to assess the full range of changes.

bills.who_affected: U.S. military veterans, surviving spouses of veterans, VA benefits claimants, VA administrative staff, veterans service organizations
bills.ticker_alert: ⚖️ New bill proposes improvements to veterans' federal benefits claims process

bills.title: Taxpayer Funds Oversight and Accountability Act
bills.plain_title: Federal Spending Oversight and Accountability Act
bills.deck: This bill would strengthen oversight of how the federal government spends taxpayer money.
bills.plain_summary: The Taxpayer Funds Oversight and Accountability Act (HR 8340) aims to increase transparency and accountability in how the federal government spends public money. It was referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, meaning it is still in the early stages of the legislative process and has not yet been voted on. The bill reflects ongoing congressional efforts to reduce waste, fraud, and abuse in federal spending.

impacts.text: Could add bureaucratic reporting requirements that slow down legitimate government programs and service delivery
May help reduce waste and fraud in federal spending, potentially saving taxpayers billions of dollars over time
The actual effectiveness depends heavily on the specific enforcement mechanisms written into the bill, which are not yet widely detailed

bills.who_affected: Federal agency employees, government contractors, taxpayers, recipients of federal grants and programs, congressional oversight staff
bills.ticker_alert: 💰 New bill aims to boost oversight of how your tax dollars are spent

bills.title: 6PPD Task Force Act
bills.plain_title: Task Force to Study Tire Chemical Harming Salmon
bills.deck: Creates a federal task force to study and address 6PPD, a tire rubber chemical killing salmon and other fish.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would establish a federal task force to study 6PPD-quinone, a toxic chemical produced when a common tire preservative reacts with ozone. The chemical washes off roads into waterways and has been linked to mass deaths of coho salmon and other fish. The bill was referred to multiple House committees and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Potential costs to tire manufacturers and drivers if task force findings lead to regulations requiring reformulated tires
Could protect salmon populations and drinking water sources by identifying actionable solutions to a known toxic chemical threat
The task force only studies the problem and makes recommendations — it does not directly ban or regulate 6PPD chemicals

bills.who_affected: Commercial and recreational fishermen, Pacific Northwest communities, tire manufacturers, environmental agencies, Native American tribes with fishing treaty rights, wildlife conservationists
bills.ticker_alert: 🐟 Congress eyes toxic tire chemical devastating salmon populations nationwide

bills.title: Breast Cancer Research Stamp Reauthorization Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Keep the Breast Cancer Awareness Stamp Going
bills.deck: This bill extends the special postage stamp that raises money for breast cancer research.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would reauthorize the Breast Cancer Research semipostal stamp, which lets Americans pay a little extra when buying postage, with the extra money going to breast cancer research funding. The stamp has raised hundreds of millions of dollars since 1998 and requires periodic reauthorization by Congress to keep selling. The bill has been referred to multiple House committees for review before any floor vote.

impacts.text: Buyers pay slightly above face value for the stamp, meaning it costs more than standard postage for everyday mailers.
Extends a proven fundraising tool that has generated over $100 million for breast cancer research, potentially saving lives.
Critics question whether disease-specific fundraising through postal stamps is the most efficient or equitable way to direct medical research dollars.

bills.who_affected: Breast cancer patients and survivors, medical researchers, everyday stamp buyers, postal customers, veterans (via Armed Services committee jurisdiction)
bills.ticker_alert: 🎗️ Congress considers extending breast cancer research stamp fundraising program

bills.title: SAFER Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Bank Account Access Rules for Cannabis Businesses
bills.deck: This bill would allow legal cannabis businesses to access banking and financial services without federal penalties.
bills.plain_summary: The SAFER Act of 2026 (HR 8338) would protect banks and credit unions from federal punishment if they provide accounts, loans, or other financial services to cannabis businesses operating legally under state law. Currently, most banks refuse to serve cannabis companies because marijuana remains illegal federally, forcing many to operate as cash-only businesses. This bill has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services, where it awaits further consideration.

impacts.text: Critics argue it could expand cannabis access and use by making the industry more financially stable and legitimate without fully resolving the federal-state legal conflict.
Legal cannabis businesses and their employees could gain access to checking accounts, payroll services, and loans, reducing dangerous cash-only operations and improving worker safety.
The bill does not legalize cannabis federally — it only addresses banking access, leaving the broader debate over marijuana's legal status unresolved.

bills.who_affected: Cannabis business owners, dispensary employees, banks and credit unions, law enforcement, state tax authorities, small business owners in cannabis-legal states
bills.ticker_alert: 🏦 SAFER Act would open banks to legal cannabis businesses nationwide

bills.title: FIGHT Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Farm and Food Import Trade Enforcement Act
bills.deck: This bill strengthens enforcement against unfair foreign agricultural imports that undercut American farmers.
bills.plain_summary: The FIGHT Act of 2025 (HR 3946) aims to protect American farmers by cracking down on foreign agricultural products that enter the U.S. market through unfair trade practices, such as subsidies or dumping below cost. The bill would give federal agencies stronger tools to investigate and restrict these imports. It was referred to the House Committee on Agriculture, where it awaits review before any further action.

impacts.text: Stricter import restrictions could raise grocery prices for American consumers if cheaper foreign food products are blocked or taxed.
American farmers could see fairer competition and higher prices for their crops if foreign dumping and subsidized imports are curbed.
Stronger trade enforcement could prompt retaliatory measures from trading partners, affecting U.S. agricultural export markets as well.

bills.who_affected: American farmers and ranchers, food importers and distributors, grocery consumers, agricultural trade workers, foreign food exporters
bills.ticker_alert: 🌾 FIGHT Act targets unfair foreign farm imports to protect U.S. agriculture

bills.title: Women and Lung Cancer Research and Preventive Services Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Lung Cancer Research and Prevention for Women Act
bills.deck: Expands Medicare coverage for lung cancer screenings and boosts research funding for women with lung cancer.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require Medicare to cover lung cancer screenings and related preventive services for women without cost-sharing, meaning no copays or deductibles. It also directs federal agencies to increase research into why lung cancer affects women differently than men. The bill passed the House and its latest action suggests it moved through procedural steps, though Senate passage would still be needed to become law.

impacts.text: Expanded Medicare coverage could increase federal spending, potentially adding to the national deficit over time.
Women on Medicare could receive lung cancer screenings at no out-of-pocket cost, enabling earlier detection and better survival rates.
The bill focuses research dollars specifically on women, which some say corrects a historical gap while others argue it narrows broader cancer research priorities.

bills.who_affected: Women aged 50 and older, Medicare beneficiaries, lung cancer patients, oncology researchers, public health agencies
bills.ticker_alert: 🩺 House passes bill expanding free lung cancer screenings for women on Medicare

bills.title: To amend the Public Health Service Act to reauthorize the telehealth network and telehealth resource centers grant programs.
bills.plain_title: Renewing Federal Grants for Telehealth Networks
bills.deck: This bill extends federal funding for programs that help rural and underserved communities access healthcare via technology.
bills.plain_summary: This bill reauthorizes two federal grant programs under the Public Health Service Act that support telehealth networks and resource centers — organizations that help clinics, hospitals, and patients in rural or underserved areas use video and remote healthcare technology. By renewing these grants, Congress would continue funding the infrastructure and training needed to expand telemedicine access. The bill has passed its final procedural step in Congress, meaning it has been agreed to without objection.

impacts.text: Continued federal spending on telehealth grants adds to the national budget without guaranteed measurable outcomes or cost-effectiveness reviews.
Patients in rural and low-income communities gain continued access to remote medical care, reducing travel burdens and improving health outcomes.
The bill reauthorizes existing programs rather than creating new ones, so its real-world effect depends heavily on how grant funds are distributed and used by recipients.

bills.who_affected: Rural residents, low-income patients, community health centers, telehealth providers, medical professionals in underserved areas
bills.ticker_alert: 📡 Congress renews telehealth grants to expand rural and underserved healthcare access

bills.title: Improving Care in Rural America Reauthorization Act of 2025
bills.plain_title: Renewing Rural Healthcare Support Programs
bills.deck: Reauthorizes federal programs that help rural communities access better healthcare services.
bills.plain_summary: This bill renews existing federal programs designed to improve healthcare access and quality in rural areas of the United States. It extends funding and support for initiatives that help rural hospitals, clinics, and health workers serve communities that often struggle to attract medical professionals and resources. The bill passed the House and its motion to reconsider was laid on the table, meaning it has cleared an early legislative hurdle.

impacts.text: Reauthorization without program reforms may continue funding ineffective or outdated rural health initiatives without accountability improvements.
Rural residents gain continued access to federally supported health services, reducing care gaps in underserved communities.
The bill extends existing programs rather than creating new ones, so its real-world effect depends heavily on how agencies implement and fund the programs.

bills.who_affected: Rural residents, rural hospital staff, rural primary care physicians, rural community health workers, small-town patients, federally qualified health center employees
bills.ticker_alert: 🏥 Rural healthcare programs renewed — access extended for millions in small towns

bills.title: Commission on Advancing Restorative Justice in Elementary and Secondary Education Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Study Commission on Restorative Justice in K-12 Schools
bills.deck: Creates a federal commission to study replacing school suspensions with restorative justice practices.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would establish a federal commission to study the use of restorative justice programs in K-12 public schools as alternatives to traditional discipline like suspensions and expulsions. Restorative justice focuses on repairing harm and keeping students in school rather than removing them. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce, where it awaits further action.

impacts.text: Critics worry reduced suspensions could leave teachers and students less safe if disruptive or harmful behavior goes insufficiently addressed
Students — especially minorities who face disproportionate suspension rates — could benefit from approaches that keep them in school and on track to graduate
The commission would only study and recommend policies, not mandate any school discipline changes, making real-world impact uncertain

bills.who_affected: K-12 public school students, teachers and school staff, school administrators, parents, minority and low-income student communities
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 New bill proposes federal study of restorative justice in K-12 schools

bills.title: Transit Workforce Development Act
bills.plain_title: Training Program for Public Transit Workers
bills.deck: Creates federal grants to train and develop a skilled workforce for public transit systems nationwide.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would establish a federal grant program to help public transit agencies recruit, train, and retain workers, including bus drivers, mechanics, and rail operators. It aims to address staffing shortages that have reduced service in many cities. The bill was referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure, meaning it has not yet been debated or voted on.

impacts.text: Creates new federal spending and bureaucracy that critics say duplicates existing workforce programs already funded by transit agencies.
Riders in understaffed transit systems could see more frequent and reliable bus and train service as agencies fill open positions.
Grant distribution formulas will determine which cities and transit agencies benefit most, creating winners and losers among regions.

bills.who_affected: Public transit riders, transit agency employees, job seekers in transportation sector, urban and suburban commuters, local transit agencies
bills.ticker_alert: 🚌 New bill would fund training programs for public transit workers nationwide

bills.title: Keep Public Funds in Public Schools Act
bills.plain_title: Ban Federal Tax Breaks for Private School Voucher Programs
bills.deck: This bill would eliminate federal tax deductions for donations that fund private school voucher programs.
bills.plain_summary: The Keep Public Funds in Public Schools Act would remove federal tax incentives for contributions to scholarship programs that send students to private or religious schools using public-adjacent funding. It targets so-called 'neo-voucher' programs where donors receive state tax credits for giving money to private school scholarship funds. The bill was introduced in the U.S. Senate and has been referred to the Senate Finance Committee, where most tax-related legislation is first reviewed.

impacts.text: Families relying on private school scholarships funded by donor tax incentives could lose access to those educational options if donation funding dries up.
Supporters say it would protect public school funding by closing a loophole that effectively redirects tax dollars away from public education systems.
The bill reignites a long-standing debate over school choice versus public school investment, with contested evidence on how voucher programs affect student outcomes.

bills.who_affected: Public school students and teachers, private and religious school students, low-income families using school vouchers, taxpayers, state education departments, donors to scholarship organizations
bills.ticker_alert: 🏫 Senate bill would strip federal tax breaks from private school voucher donations

bills.title: Communications, Video, and Technology Accessibility Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Disability Access Rules for TV, Internet, and Tech Services
bills.deck: This bill updates federal rules to ensure people with disabilities can access modern communications and video technology.
bills.plain_summary: The Communications, Video, and Technology Accessibility Act of 2026 would modernize accessibility requirements for television, internet-based video, and communications services so that people with disabilities — including those who are deaf, hard of hearing, blind, or have mobility impairments — can fully use them. It aims to update outdated laws to cover newer streaming platforms and digital technologies. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process.

impacts.text: Compliance costs for tech companies and smaller streaming services could increase, potentially raising subscription prices for all consumers.
Millions of Americans with visual, hearing, or mobility disabilities would gain improved access to streaming video, apps, and online communications tools.
The bill would expand the FCC's regulatory authority over newer digital and streaming platforms, which supporters call overdue and critics call government overreach.

bills.who_affected: Deaf and hard-of-hearing Americans, blind and low-vision Americans, people with mobility or cognitive disabilities, streaming service providers, telecom companies, app developers, seniors with age-related disabilities
bills.ticker_alert: ♿ Bill aims to modernize disability access rules for streaming and digital tech

bills.title: Clean Slate through Rehabilitation Act
bills.plain_title: Second Chance Job Training for People with Criminal Records
bills.deck: This bill would fund job training and education programs for people with criminal records to help them find stable employment.
bills.plain_summary: The Clean Slate through Rehabilitation Act would create or expand federal funding for workforce training, education, and rehabilitation programs aimed at people who have been convicted of crimes. The goal is to help formerly incarcerated individuals gain skills and find jobs, reducing repeat offenses. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce, meaning it is in early stages and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Critics argue federal spending on job training for convicted individuals could divert limited education funds away from law-abiding citizens seeking workforce development.
People with criminal records gain access to job training and education, improving their chances of employment and reducing likelihood of reoffending.
The effectiveness of rehabilitation-based workforce programs varies widely by implementation, making long-term outcomes difficult to predict without program specifics.

bills.who_affected: Formerly incarcerated individuals, employers hiring ex-offenders, crime victims, federal workforce training administrators, taxpayers, low-income communities with high incarceration rates
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 House bill proposes job training funding for Americans with criminal records

bills.title: DAIRY PRIDE Act
bills.plain_title: Dairy Labeling Standards: Milk Must Come From Animals
bills.deck: This bill would prohibit plant-based drinks like oat or almond milk from using the word 'milk' on their labels.
bills.plain_summary: The DAIRY PRIDE Act would require the FDA to enforce existing rules that limit the word 'milk' (and similar terms like 'cheese' and 'yogurt') to products that come from animals. Plant-based alternatives like almond, oat, or soy beverages would have to change their labeling. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce, meaning it has not yet been debated or voted on.

impacts.text: Consumers who buy plant-based beverages could face confusion and higher costs as brands are forced to relabel products they already recognize.
Dairy farmers could benefit from clearer marketplace distinctions, potentially reducing consumer confusion between animal dairy and plant-based alternatives.
The bill reignites a long-running debate over whether shared food terms mislead consumers or simply reflect common, widely understood language.

bills.who_affected: Dairy farmers, plant-based food manufacturers, grocery retailers, lactose-intolerant consumers, vegans and vegetarians, FDA regulators
bills.ticker_alert: 🥛 Bill would ban 'milk' label on oat, almond, and soy drinks

bills.title: Ban Presidential Plunder of Taxpayer Funds Act
bills.plain_title: Ban on Presidents Profiting from Taxpayer Money
bills.deck: This bill would prohibit presidents and vice presidents from personally profiting from federal government spending.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would make it illegal for a sitting president or vice president to personally benefit financially from federal taxpayer funds, such as through government contracts or payments to their businesses. It was introduced in the U.S. Senate and has been sent to the Senate Judiciary Committee for review. The bill has not yet been voted on and faces an uncertain path forward.

impacts.text: Critics say the bill's enforcement mechanisms may be difficult to implement and could invite politically motivated legal battles against executives.
Taxpayers would gain stronger legal protections ensuring their money cannot flow back to businesses owned or controlled by the sitting president or vice president.
Courts and legal experts would likely debate what counts as 'profiting' from government funds, creating potential ambiguity in enforcement.

bills.who_affected: U.S. taxpayers, current and future presidents and vice presidents, federal contractors, ethics watchdog organizations, Congress members
bills.ticker_alert: 🚫 Senate bill would ban presidents from profiting off taxpayer dollars

bills.title: Directing the President, pursuant to section 5(c) of the War Powers Resolution, to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities with Iran.
bills.plain_title: Order U.S. Troops Removed from Iran Hostilities
bills.deck: This bill directs the President to withdraw U.S. military forces from any armed conflict with Iran.
bills.plain_summary: This concurrent resolution uses the War Powers Resolution to formally order the President to pull U.S. Armed Forces out of hostilities involving Iran. It was referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs, which is the first step in the legislative process. The bill reflects congressional concern that military action against Iran may be occurring without a formal declaration of war or proper congressional authorization.

impacts.text: Critics argue it could weaken U.S. deterrence against Iran, potentially emboldening Iranian aggression toward American allies and troops in the region.
Could prevent an unauthorized escalation into a full-scale war with Iran, reducing risk to U.S. military personnel and avoiding massive economic costs.
The bill reignites a longstanding constitutional debate over whether Congress or the President holds primary authority to commit U.S. forces to hostilities.

bills.who_affected: U.S. military service members and their families, American civilians in the Middle East, U.S. allies in the region, defense contractors, Members of Congress
bills.ticker_alert: ⚠️ Congress seeks to order President to halt U.S. military hostilities with Iran

bills.title: TALENTS Act
bills.plain_title: Hire Veterans for Federal Government Jobs Act
