bills.title: Survivor Justice Tax Prevention Act
bills.plain_title: Tax Relief for Sexual Abuse and Trafficking Survivors
bills.deck: Prevents the IRS from taxing lawsuit settlements received by survivors of sexual abuse or human trafficking.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would make financial settlements received by survivors of sexual abuse, assault, or human trafficking fully tax-free, even when the money is paid by a third party like an employer or institution. Under current law, some survivor compensation can be subject to federal income tax depending on how the settlement is structured. The bill has been placed on the Union Calendar, meaning it has cleared committee and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Reduces federal tax revenue by exempting certain settlement payments, potentially a small but real budget cost
Survivors of abuse and trafficking keep their full settlement amount rather than losing a portion to federal income taxes
The bill's benefit is tied to who pays the settlement — critics may debate whether third-party payers like corporations deserve the same tax treatment

bills.who_affected: Sexual abuse survivors, human trafficking victims, personal injury attorneys, employers and institutions that settle abuse claims, IRS
bills.ticker_alert: 💰 Bill would make abuse and trafficking survivor settlements fully tax-free

bills.title: Impacts and Outcomes for Health Career Training Act
bills.plain_title: Health Career Training Program Transparency and Outcomes Act
bills.deck: Requires health career training programs to report student outcome data to improve accountability and transparency.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require health career training programs to track and report data on student outcomes, such as job placement rates and earnings after graduation. The goal is to help prospective students make better-informed decisions about which programs are worth their time and money. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means, meaning it is in the early stages of the legislative process.

impacts.text: Smaller or community-based health training programs may face costly new reporting burdens that strain limited administrative resources.
Prospective students can compare real job placement and earnings data before enrolling, reducing risk of wasted tuition on low-value programs.
The quality and comparability of reported outcome data will depend heavily on how the federal government standardizes and enforces the reporting requirements.

bills.who_affected: Health career training students, vocational and trade school administrators, community college health programs, healthcare employers, federal education regulators
bills.ticker_alert: 📊 New bill would require health training schools to publish job placement and earnings data

bills.title: World War II Women's Memorial Location Act
bills.plain_title: WWII Women's Memorial New Location Bill
bills.deck: This bill would allow a permanent memorial honoring women who served in World War II to be built in a specific location in Washington, D.C.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would designate an approved location in Washington, D.C. for a memorial dedicated to the women who served the United States during World War II. It removes a previous restriction that limited where this memorial could be built, giving organizers more flexibility to find a fitting site. The bill has had a hearing in the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Subcommittee on National Parks but has not yet passed into law.

impacts.text: Could require additional federal review processes and use of limited National Mall space, potentially displacing other planned memorials or green areas.
Would honor the roughly 400,000 women who served in WWII, giving veterans and their families a permanent, visible place of recognition and remembrance.
The specific final location of the memorial has not been determined, meaning debates over placement, design, and funding may continue after passage.

bills.who_affected: WWII women veterans and their descendants, veterans organizations, Washington D.C. residents and tourists, National Park Service
bills.ticker_alert: 🏛️ Senate bill seeks new D.C. location for WWII Women's Memorial

bills.title: Advance Global Health Act
bills.plain_title: U.S. Global Health Program Reform and Authorization Act
bills.deck: Reorganizes and strengthens how the U.S. government funds and coordinates global health programs abroad.
bills.plain_summary: The Advance Global Health Act would update and authorize U.S. government efforts to fight diseases, improve health systems, and respond to health crises in other countries. It aims to make American global health spending more strategic and accountable. The bill passed its committee vote overwhelmingly, 41 to 2, and now awaits a full House vote.

impacts.text: Critics argue it continues directing billions in taxpayer dollars to foreign health programs rather than domestic needs.
Stronger global disease prevention could reduce the risk of future pandemics reaching American shores, protecting U.S. public health.
The bill restructures existing global health bureaucracy, which may shift priorities and funding allocations among current international programs.

bills.who_affected: U.S. foreign aid workers, global health NGOs receiving federal grants, State Department and USAID employees, American pharmaceutical and public health contractors, taxpayers
bills.ticker_alert: 🌍 House panel advances bill to overhaul U.S. global health foreign aid programs

bills.title: Rejecting the Erasure of Afghan Women and Girls Act
bills.plain_title: U.S. Policy Against Taliban Oppression of Afghan Women
bills.deck: This bill directs the U.S. government to formally oppose Taliban policies that erase Afghan women and girls from public life.
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires the U.S. government to officially reject Taliban policies that ban Afghan women and girls from education, work, and public spaces. It directs U.S. diplomats and agencies to treat these Taliban restrictions as a form of gender-based persecution and to make opposing them a priority in foreign policy. The bill passed committee with overwhelming bipartisan support (44-2) and is awaiting a full House vote.

impacts.text: Critics argue the bill adds diplomatic pressure without enforcement tools, potentially straining negotiations needed for other U.S. interests in the region.
Human rights advocates say the bill strengthens U.S. moral authority and gives diplomats a clearer mandate to push back against Taliban gender oppression.
The bill is largely symbolic in its direct reach, directing policy statements and diplomatic posture rather than imposing new sanctions or funding measures.

bills.who_affected: U.S. foreign policy officials and diplomats, Afghan-American communities, human rights organizations, women's advocacy groups, NGOs operating in Afghanistan
bills.ticker_alert: 🌐 Congress moves to formally oppose Taliban's ban on Afghan women's rights

bills.title: Venezuela Democratic Transition Strategy Act
bills.plain_title: US Strategy Plan for Venezuela's Democratic Transition
bills.deck: Requires the U.S. government to develop a formal strategy supporting a democratic transition in Venezuela.
bills.plain_summary: This bill directs the State Department and other agencies to create a comprehensive strategy for supporting democracy in Venezuela, including coordinating international pressure on the Maduro government and assisting Venezuelan civil society. It was introduced in the House and passed committee with strong bipartisan support, 41-5. The bill reflects ongoing U.S. concerns about authoritarian rule and humanitarian crisis in Venezuela.

impacts.text: Could draw the U.S. into deeper foreign entanglements, potentially costing taxpayer dollars on foreign policy initiatives with uncertain outcomes.
A stable, democratic Venezuela could reduce migration pressure to the U.S. and restore a regional trade and energy partner.
The strategy is primarily a planning requirement and does not by itself authorize military action or major new spending, leaving real impact uncertain.

bills.who_affected: Venezuelan-American communities, U.S. foreign policy and diplomatic staff, American energy companies with Venezuela interests, U.S. border and immigration officials
bills.ticker_alert: 🌎 Congress pushes formal U.S. strategy to restore democracy in Venezuela

bills.title: No Funding to Honor Crime Scenes Act
bills.plain_title: Ban Federal Funds for Memorials at Mass Shooting Sites
bills.deck: This bill would prohibit federal money from being used to create memorials or monuments at locations where mass shootings occurred.
bills.plain_summary: The 'No Funding to Honor Crime Scenes Act' would ban the use of federal funds to build, maintain, or support any memorial or monument at a site where a mass shooting took place. The bill was referred to the Senate Committee on Energy and Natural Resources, suggesting it may apply particularly to federally managed lands or national parks. It has not yet moved beyond the committee stage and would need to pass both chambers of Congress before becoming law.

impacts.text: Families and communities affected by mass shootings may be denied federal support to memorialize victims at the locations where tragedies occurred.
Supporters argue this prevents taxpayer dollars from being used to permanently mark crime scenes, which some believe could glorify or institutionalize sites of violence.
The bill raises unresolved questions about how 'mass shooting' is defined and whether existing memorials on federal land would be affected or removed.

bills.who_affected: Mass shooting survivors, victims' families, local communities near shooting sites, federal land managers, taxpayers, memorial advocates
bills.ticker_alert: 🚫 Senate bill would ban federal funds for mass shooting site memorials

bills.title: Special Operations Forces Concealed Carry Act
bills.plain_title: Concealed Carry Rights for Special Operations Veterans
bills.deck: This bill would allow retired special operations forces members to carry concealed firearms nationwide.
bills.plain_summary: HR 8332 would grant current and former U.S. Special Operations Forces members — such as Navy SEALs and Army Rangers — the right to carry concealed firearms in all 50 states, similar to rights already extended to law enforcement officers under existing federal law. The bill was referred to the House Committees on Judiciary, Armed Services, and Veterans' Affairs for review. It has not yet been voted on and remains in the early committee stage.

impacts.text: Critics argue it creates a privileged class of gun carriers, bypassing state concealed carry laws and local public safety decisions.
Supporters say it recognizes the elite training of special operations veterans and helps them protect themselves and others after service.
The bill raises unresolved questions about how 'special operations forces' would be defined and verified, which could affect implementation.

bills.who_affected: Active and retired special operations military personnel, state law enforcement agencies, gun rights advocates, gun control advocates, veterans organizations
bills.ticker_alert: 🔫 Bill proposes nationwide concealed carry rights for special operations veterans

bills.title: A resolution designating the week beginning February 2, 2026, as "National Tribal Colleges and Universities Week".
bills.plain_title: National Tribal Colleges and Universities Awareness Week
bills.deck: This resolution officially designates one week in February 2026 to honor tribal colleges and universities across America.
bills.plain_summary: This Senate resolution designates the week of February 2, 2026, as 'National Tribal Colleges and Universities Week' to recognize the importance of tribal colleges and universities (TCUs) in serving Native American communities. It is a symbolic measure with no funding or legal mandates attached. The resolution passed the Senate unanimously by consent, meaning no formal vote was needed.

impacts.text: The resolution is purely symbolic and allocates no funding or resources to address the chronic underfunding many tribal colleges face.
Raises national awareness of tribal colleges and universities, potentially boosting public support, enrollment interest, and future legislative attention for these institutions.
Symbolic resolutions like this have no binding legal effect but are sometimes used as stepping stones toward more substantive legislation.

bills.who_affected: Native American and Alaska Native students, tribal college faculty and staff, tribal nations, Indigenous communities, prospective students considering tribal colleges
bills.ticker_alert: 🎓 Senate unanimously honors tribal colleges with official awareness week designation

bills.title: Golden Thirteen Congressional Gold Medal Act
bills.plain_title: Gold Medal Honor for First Black Navy Officers
bills.deck: Awards the Congressional Gold Medal to the Golden Thirteen, the first Black officers commissioned in the U.S. Navy.
bills.plain_summary: This bill proposes awarding the Congressional Gold Medal — Congress's highest civilian honor — to the Golden Thirteen, the group of 13 African American men who became the first Black officers commissioned in the U.S. Navy in 1944. The bill recognizes their historic role in breaking racial barriers in the military during World War II. It has been referred to the House Financial Services and House Administration committees for review.

impacts.text: Minimal fiscal cost to produce the medal, but critics note symbolic honors don't address ongoing racial disparities in the military.
Formally recognizes a historically overlooked milestone in civil rights and military history, preserving it in the national record.
The medal is largely ceremonial with no direct policy changes; its broader cultural significance is debated.

bills.who_affected: African American veterans and their descendants, U.S. Navy personnel, historians, civil rights advocates, World War II commemorative communities
bills.ticker_alert: 🏅 Congress moves to honor first Black U.S. Navy officers with Gold Medal

bills.title: A resolution condemning the Government of Iran's state-sponsored persecution of the Baha'i minority and its continued violation of the International Covenants on Human Rights.
bills.plain_title: Senate Condemnation of Iran's Persecution of Baha'i People
bills.deck: This resolution formally condemns Iran's government for persecuting its Baha'i religious minority and violating international human rights law.
bills.plain_summary: This Senate resolution formally expresses the U.S. Senate's condemnation of Iran's ongoing persecution of the Baha'i faith community, including arrests, executions, and denial of education and employment. It calls attention to Iran's violations of international human rights agreements it has signed. The resolution has been referred to the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, where it awaits further consideration. Resolutions like this are symbolic statements and do not create new law or policy.

impacts.text: Could further strain U.S.-Iran diplomatic relations, potentially complicating nuclear talks or hostage negotiations.
Gives American Baha'i communities and human rights advocates official congressional backing for their advocacy efforts.
As a non-binding resolution, it carries moral weight but imposes no legal obligations on Iran or changes U.S. foreign policy.

bills.who_affected: American Baha'i community members, Iranian-Americans, human rights advocates, U.S. foreign policy professionals, diplomats
bills.ticker_alert: 🕊️ Senate condemns Iran's persecution of Baha'i religious minority in symbolic resolution

bills.title: National Cemetery Access Act
bills.plain_title: Improving Veterans' Access to National Cemetery Services
bills.deck: This bill aims to make it easier for veterans and families to access national cemetery burial benefits and services.
bills.plain_summary: The National Cemetery Access Act seeks to improve how veterans and their eligible family members can access burial services at national cemeteries, potentially addressing transportation, scheduling, or logistical barriers. The bill was referred to the House Subcommittee on Disability Assistance and Memorial Affairs, meaning it is in early stages of review. Without full bill text publicly available, the specifics of how access would be improved remain subject to the subcommittee's examination.

impacts.text: Implementation costs could draw from limited VA budget resources, potentially affecting other veteran services.
Veterans and families in rural or underserved areas may find it easier to access burial honors and cemetery services they are entitled to.
The bill's effectiveness depends heavily on how 'access' is defined and funded, which has not yet been publicly detailed.

bills.who_affected: Veterans, surviving spouses of veterans, eligible family members of deceased veterans, VA cemetery staff, rural communities near national cemeteries
bills.ticker_alert: 🪖 Bill introduced to improve veterans' access to national cemetery burial services

bills.title: A resolution designating September 2025 as "National Voting Rights Month".
bills.plain_title: Designate September 2025 as National Voting Rights Month
bills.deck: This resolution would officially recognize September 2025 as a month to raise awareness about Americans' voting rights.
bills.plain_summary: This is a Senate resolution that would designate September 2025 as 'National Voting Rights Month' to highlight the importance of voting rights in the United States. It is a symbolic measure, meaning it does not create new laws, spend money, or change any existing rules. The resolution has been referred to the Senate Judiciary Committee, where it awaits further review.

impacts.text: Critics may argue it uses congressional time on symbolic gestures rather than passing enforceable voting rights legislation.
Could raise public awareness about voting rights protections and encourage civic participation among eligible voters nationwide.
As a non-binding resolution, it carries no legal force and creates no new rights, obligations, or government programs.

bills.who_affected: Eligible voters, voting rights advocates, minority communities with historically restricted ballot access, civic education organizations
bills.ticker_alert: 🗳️ Senate resolution proposes September 2025 as National Voting Rights Month

bills.title: Gold Star and Surviving Spouse Career Services Act
bills.plain_title: Career Help for Military Widows and Gold Star Families
bills.deck: This bill would provide career counseling and job placement services to surviving spouses and families of fallen service members.
bills.plain_summary: The Gold Star and Surviving Spouse Career Services Act would direct the Department of Labor or a related agency to offer dedicated career services, including job training and employment assistance, to Gold Star family members and surviving spouses of deceased military personnel. The bill aims to help these individuals rebuild financial stability after losing a loved one in military service. It has been introduced in the House and has reached the subcommittee hearing stage, meaning it is still in early review.

impacts.text: Creating a dedicated program may require new federal funding or administrative overhead, adding to government expenditures with uncertain cost-effectiveness.
Surviving military spouses and Gold Star family members would gain access to targeted job training and employment support during a difficult financial transition.
The bill's reach is narrow, affecting a relatively small population, so broader debates focus on whether dedicated programs outperform existing veteran and workforce services.

bills.who_affected: Surviving spouses of fallen service members, Gold Star family members, Department of Labor workforce staff, federal budget planners
bills.ticker_alert: 🎖️ Bill proposes career services for Gold Star families and military surviving spouses

bills.title: Credit Union Board Modernization Act
bills.plain_title: Allow Credit Unions to Meet Less Often
bills.deck: This bill lets credit union boards hold fewer required meetings each year.
bills.plain_summary: The Credit Union Board Modernization Act would reduce how often federally chartered credit union boards of directors are required to meet, giving them more scheduling flexibility. The bill passed the House and was sent to the Senate Banking Committee for review. It aims to reduce administrative burdens on volunteer board members who run these nonprofit financial institutions.

impacts.text: Less frequent board meetings could reduce oversight of credit union finances, potentially increasing risk of mismanagement going undetected.
Volunteer board members face less scheduling pressure, making it easier to recruit qualified people to govern community credit unions.
Credit unions would still set their own meeting schedules; this bill simply removes a federal minimum frequency requirement.

bills.who_affected: Credit union members, credit union volunteer board directors, federally chartered credit union staff, community banking customers
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 Bill would let credit union boards skip some required annual meetings

bills.title: DPA Private-Sector Outreach Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Defense Production Act Private Sector Outreach Requirement
bills.deck: Requires the government to consult with private businesses when using emergency industrial powers.
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires federal officials to conduct outreach to private sector companies before invoking the Defense Production Act, a law that gives the government broad powers to direct industries during national emergencies. It aims to ensure businesses have input before the government mandates production priorities or allocates resources. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: Adding a consultation requirement could slow the government's ability to respond quickly in fast-moving national emergencies or supply chain crises.
Businesses would get advance notice and a voice before the government issues mandates, reducing economic disruption and legal uncertainty for manufacturers.
The bill defines a new procedural step but does not limit what the government can ultimately do under the Defense Production Act once outreach occurs.

bills.who_affected: Defense contractors, manufacturers, pharmaceutical companies, critical materials producers, small businesses in defense supply chains, federal emergency planners
bills.ticker_alert: 📋 New bill would require gov't to consult businesses before invoking emergency production powers

bills.title: DPA Advanced Procurement Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Defense Production Act Advanced Buying Authority Bill
bills.deck: This bill would allow the government to buy defense materials earlier using Defense Production Act powers.
bills.plain_summary: The DPA Advanced Procurement Act of 2026 (HR 8136) would expand the government's authority under the Defense Production Act to make advance purchases of critical materials, components, and supplies needed for national defense before they are immediately needed. This approach is intended to strengthen supply chains and reduce shortages during emergencies or conflicts. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services, meaning it is in an early stage of the legislative process.

impacts.text: Taxpayers could bear financial risk if pre-purchased materials become obsolete, are overpriced, or go unused due to changing defense needs.
American manufacturers in the defense supply chain could receive more stable, earlier contracts, supporting domestic jobs and industrial readiness.
The scope of what qualifies as a 'critical' defense material eligible for advance procurement remains subject to interpretation and agency discretion.

bills.who_affected: Defense contractors, domestic manufacturers, U.S. taxpayers, Pentagon procurement officials, defense supply chain workers
bills.ticker_alert: 🛡️ Bill would let Pentagon pre-buy defense supplies before shortages hit

bills.title: DPA Specialized Staffing Act
bills.plain_title: Hire Defense Production Act Specialists in Federal Agencies
bills.deck: Requires federal agencies to hire dedicated staff experts to manage Defense Production Act authorities.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would require certain federal agencies to employ specialized staff who are trained and focused on using the Defense Production Act (DPA), a law that lets the government direct private companies to produce critical goods during national emergencies. The goal is to make sure agencies have experts ready to quickly mobilize industrial production when crises arise, such as during pandemics or supply chain disruptions. The bill was referred to two House committees and has not yet advanced further.

impacts.text: Adds new federal personnel costs and bureaucratic layers that taxpayers must fund, even outside of emergencies.
Could speed up government response during crises like pandemics or shortages, getting critical supplies to Americans faster.
The effectiveness of specialized staffing depends heavily on how agencies implement the requirement and whether emergencies actually occur.

bills.who_affected: Federal agency employees, defense contractors, manufacturers, emergency management officials, taxpayers, Americans reliant on critical goods during crises
bills.ticker_alert: 🏭 Bill would require federal agencies to hire Defense Production Act specialists

bills.title: DPA Workforce and Skilled Labor Needs Act of 2026
bills.plain_title: Defense Production Act Workforce Training and Labor Study
bills.deck: Requires a study on skilled labor shortages affecting U.S. defense manufacturing and critical industries.
bills.plain_summary: This bill directs a formal assessment of workforce and skilled labor gaps in industries covered by the Defense Production Act, which governs U.S. production of critical goods like weapons, semiconductors, and medical supplies. The goal is to identify shortages and inform future policy solutions for defense and national security manufacturing. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Financial Services and has not yet been voted on.

impacts.text: A study alone does not fund job training programs, meaning workers may wait years before any real help arrives.
Identifying specific skilled labor gaps could lead to targeted investments that create well-paying manufacturing and defense jobs for Americans.
The bill's findings could be used to argue for expanding apprenticeship programs, union workforce pipelines, or corporate training subsidies — a contested policy debate.

bills.who_affected: Defense manufacturing workers, semiconductor and tech industry employees, military contractors, vocational and trade school students, rural and industrial communities
bills.ticker_alert: 🔍 Congress orders study on skilled worker shortages in U.S. defense industries

bills.title: Electing a Member to a certain standing committee of the House of Representatives.
bills.plain_title: House Committee Seat Assignment Resolution
bills.deck: This resolution assigns a specific House member to fill a vacant seat on a standing committee.
bills.plain_summary: This resolution formally elects a member of the House of Representatives to serve on a specific standing committee. Committee assignments are routine procedural actions that determine which lawmakers oversee particular policy areas like defense, finance, or education. The resolution has already passed, as the motion to reconsider was laid on the table without objection.

impacts.text: Reshuffling committee membership can disrupt ongoing legislative work or shift a committee's ideological balance in ways voters may not prefer.
Filling a committee vacancy ensures full representation and working capacity on oversight and legislative duties that affect Americans.
Committee assignments reflect internal House power dynamics and party negotiations, which can influence which bills get hearings or votes.

bills.who_affected: Members of Congress, constituents of the assigned representative, stakeholders in the policy areas the committee oversees
bills.ticker_alert: 🏛️ House assigns member to standing committee in routine procedural vote

bills.title: To change the address of the postal facility designated in honor of Specialist Jeffrey L. White, Jr.
bills.plain_title: Update Address for Specialist Jeffrey L. White Jr. Post Office
bills.deck: This bill updates the official address of a post office named after a fallen soldier.
bills.plain_summary: This bill corrects or updates the street address associated with the postal facility that was previously dedicated in honor of Specialist Jeffrey L. White Jr., a U.S. military servicemember. It is a housekeeping measure to ensure official records accurately reflect the facility's current address. The bill was referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform and has not yet advanced further.

impacts.text: Requires administrative work and minor government resources to update official records and signage.
Ensures the memorial dedication to a fallen soldier is accurately recorded and properly honored in official documents.
This is a largely procedural change with no policy implications beyond correcting a government record.

bills.who_affected: U.S. Postal Service staff at the affected facility, residents of the local community, family of Specialist Jeffrey L. White Jr.
bills.ticker_alert: 📬 Bill updates address for post office honoring fallen U.S. soldier

bills.title: Supporting the designation of May as "National Myositis Awareness Month".
bills.plain_title: Declare May as National Myositis Awareness Month
bills.deck: This resolution asks Congress to officially recognize May as the awareness month for myositis, a rare inflammatory muscle disease.
bills.plain_summary: This House resolution supports designating May as 'National Myositis Awareness Month' to raise public awareness about myositis, a group of rare diseases that cause muscle inflammation and weakness. It is a symbolic resolution with no binding legal force or spending attached. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce, where most such resolutions begin their review process.

impacts.text: The resolution has no funding or enforcement mechanism, so it may raise expectations without delivering real resources for patients or researchers.
Increased public awareness could help the roughly 75,000 Americans with myositis get faster diagnoses and encourage more research funding and donations.
Awareness month designations are largely symbolic and do not change law, funding levels, or healthcare policy for myositis patients.

bills.who_affected: Myositis patients, their families and caregivers, rheumatologists and neurologists, rare disease advocacy organizations, medical researchers
bills.ticker_alert: 📢 Congress considers declaring May National Myositis Awareness Month

bills.title: Expressing the profound sorrow of the House of Representatives on the death of the Honorable David Scott.
bills.plain_title: House Memorial Resolution for Rep. David Scott
bills.deck: The House of Representatives formally expresses condolences on the death of Congressman David Scott.
bills.plain_summary: This resolution is a formal expression of grief from the House of Representatives following the death of Representative David Scott, who served Georgia in Congress. It is a ceremonial measure with no legal or policy effects, intended to honor his service and offer condolences to his family. The resolution has been agreed to and is considered final.

impacts.text: No negative impact on Americans; this is a purely ceremonial resolution.
Provides formal recognition of a public servant's life and legacy, honoring constituents he represented.
Such resolutions are standard congressional tradition but carry no binding legal weight or policy consequences.

bills.who_affected: Family and loved ones of Rep. David Scott, Georgia constituents he served, House of Representatives members
bills.ticker_alert: 🕊️ House passes memorial resolution honoring the late Rep. David Scott

bills.title: Directing the Architect of the Capitol to place motor vehicle gas price trackers in the Hall of the House of Representatives and the Chamber of the Senate.
bills.plain_title: Gas Price Display Boards for Congress
bills.deck: This bill would install live gasoline price trackers inside both chambers of the U.S. Capitol building.
bills.plain_summary: This concurrent resolution would require the Architect of the Capitol to install real-time motor vehicle gas price display boards in the House chamber and the Senate chamber. The idea is to keep lawmakers visually reminded of fuel costs Americans face while they debate and vote on legislation. The bill has been referred to the House Committee on House Administration, where most such proposals stall without further action.

impacts.text: Critics say it is a political stunt that wastes taxpayer money on installation and maintenance with no policy effect.
Supporters argue it could keep legislators constantly aware of energy costs burdening everyday Americans at the pump.
The displays would be symbolic only — they carry no requirement for Congress to act on gas prices.

bills.who_affected: Members of Congress, Capitol staff, American drivers and commuters, taxpayers funding Capitol operations
bills.ticker_alert: ⛽ Bill proposes live gas price boards installed inside Congress chambers

bills.title: A resolution designating March 15, 2025, as "National Osceola Turkey Day".
bills.plain_title: National Osceola Turkey Day Recognition
bills.deck: This resolution officially designates March 15, 2025, as National Osceola Turkey Day in the United States.
bills.plain_summary: This Senate resolution creates a symbolic national day honoring the Osceola wild turkey, a subspecies found in Florida and the southeastern U.S. It was passed unanimously by the Senate without any changes, meaning all senators agreed to it. The resolution has no legal enforcement power and simply serves as an official recognition of the Osceola turkey's cultural and wildlife significance.

impacts.text: Critics may argue Senate floor time was spent on a symbolic gesture rather than pressing national issues.
Raises public awareness about the Osceola turkey subspecies, potentially boosting wildlife conservation and hunting tourism in Florida.
Symbolic resolutions like this have no binding legal effect and create no new programs, spending, or obligations.

bills.who_affected: Florida hunters and wildlife enthusiasts, conservation organizations, hunting tourism businesses, wildlife biologists
bills.ticker_alert: 🦃 Senate unanimously names March 15 National Osceola Turkey Day

bills.title: A resolution recognizing and honoring National Mushroom Day and the contributions of Chester and Berks Counties to the national mushroom industry and to healthy diets.
bills.plain_title: National Mushroom Day Recognition Resolution
bills.deck: This resolution honors National Mushroom Day and recognizes Pennsylvania counties for their mushroom industry contributions.
bills.plain_summary: This is a symbolic Senate resolution that officially recognizes National Mushroom Day and celebrates the contributions of Chester and Berks Counties in Pennsylvania to the U.S. mushroom industry and healthy eating. It does not create any laws, spend any money, or impose any requirements. The resolution was referred to the Senate Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.

impacts.text: Uses limited congressional time and attention on a ceremonial measure with no policy substance
Raises public awareness of the mushroom industry and promotes healthy eating habits for all Americans
Has no legal or financial effect — it is purely symbolic recognition with no binding authority

bills.who_affected: Mushroom farmers in Pennsylvania, agricultural communities in Chester and Berks Counties, mushroom industry workers nationwide
bills.ticker_alert: 🍄 Senate honors National Mushroom Day and Pennsylvania's top mushroom-growing counties
